text: TATME for the treatment of advanced rectal cancer.
This is the case of a sixty-seven-year-old woman who received the diagnosis of rectal adenocarcinoma in February twenty eighteen.
The preoperative assessment with MRI revealed a CT-CD CN-II tumor, six centimeters from the anorectal junction, with infiltration of the mesorectal fascia on the posterior left
She underwent preoperative chemoradiotherapy, and the restaging MRI revealed a tumor volume reduction, but still a suspect of infiltration of the mesorectal fascia.
She underwent TATME with a diagnosis of rectal tumor, staged YCT-III-CD, YCN-I, YCM-O, MRF+.
The surgeon carefully dissects the posterior plane during the procedure, revealing the suspect point of infiltration in the mesorectal fascia.
The surgeon deepens the dissection into the presacral fascia to achieve a clear circumferential resection margin, ensuring no involvement of the mesorectal fascia.
The surgeon performs a careful and accurate deeper dissection in the presacral area, aiming to minimize the risk of nerve damage while resecting only the involved tissue.
The surgeon completes the dissection by reaching the end of the mesorectal fascia involvement, re-establishing the clear posterior mesorectal plane to finalize the procedure.

The final pathological exam described a T-III tumor with no involvement of the circumferential resection margin and radiation-induced fibrotic tissue reaching the mesorectal fascia mimicking an MRF-plus tumor.
